title: Haplopappus gossypinus
taxonomy: Haplopappus gossypinus is a species of flowering plant in the family Asteraceae. It is native to the southwestern United States and northern Mexico. The plant's classification includes being a member of the tribe Heliantheae and the subfamily Asteroideae. Further classification places it within the subtribe Ecliptinae. Its classification is consistent with its appearance as a shrub or subshrub.
uses: Haplopappus gossypinus, also known as the cottony hop clover, has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. Its leaves and flowers are said to have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which may be used to treat wounds and skin conditions. The plant is also believed to have antioxidant properties, which can help protect against cell damage and oxidative stress. In addition, Haplopappus gossypinus is thought to have antiseptic and astringent properties, making it a potential treatment for digestive issues. However, further research is needed to confirm its medicinal uses and potential health benefits.

intro: Haplopappus gossypinus, also known as the cottony woolly marigold or gossypine daisy, is a flowering plant species in the sunflower family (Asteraceae) native to North America. The plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, with reported health benefits including anti-inflammatory and antiseptic effects, as well as potential use in treating wounds and skin conditions. Bioactive constituents of Haplopappus gossypinus include sesquiterpene lactones, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its therapeutic properties. The plant is commonly used in herbal preparations such as infusions, tinctures, and salves, which are often employed to promote wound healing and soothe skin irritations.
